questions.question: Article 15.2.1 degrees of the Constitution vests the 'sole and exclusive power of making laws for the State' in the Oireachtas. Which of the following correctly identifies both the primary constitutional limitation on this power AND the consequence of a violation of that limitation?

options.A: Primary limitation: the Oireachtas may only legislate on matters listed in a Schedule to the Constitution (reserved powers); consequence: unlisted legislation is void but may be saved by constitutional amendment
options.B: Primary limitation: the Oireachtas may not enact any law repugnant to the Constitution (Article 15.4); consequence: such legislation is invalid and ineffective from the date of enactment (ex tunc invalidity)
options.C: Primary limitation: the Oireachtas may not enact any law without the consent of the President; consequence: unsigned legislation is of no legal effect until presidential signature is subsequently given
options.D: Primary limitation: the Oireachtas may not enact legislation that conflicts with EU law (Article 29.4); consequence: conflicting national legislation is disapplied but remains on the statute book

questions.explanation: Article 15.4.1 degrees provides that the Oireachtas shall not enact any law that is in any respect repugnant to the Constitution or any provision thereof. This is the primary constitutional limitation on legislative power. The consequence of enacting a repugnant law is that it is invalid and of no legal effect — and crucially, under the Irish approach, this invalidity operates ex tunc (from the time of enactment), not merely prospectively. This has significant practical implications: acts done under the legislation before it is struck down may also be affected. Note that the EU law limitation in Option D is accurate as a factual matter (EU primacy) but is a doctrine of EU law rather than a primary constitutional limitation of the Oireachtas's power under Art. 15.

questions.question: The Supreme Court in City View Press Ltd v An Chomhairle Oiliunna (AnCO) [1980] IR 381 articulated the test for constitutionally permissible delegation of legislative power to subordinate bodies. Which of the following most accurately states that test?

options.A: Delegation is constitutionally permissible only where the parent statute expressly states that the delegated power constitutes an extension of legislative power, with the delegated instrument being subject to automatic Oireachtas veto
options.B: The parent statute must contain the principles and policies within which the delegated body must operate; the delegated instrument (statutory instrument or order) may then give effect to those principles and policies, but may not itself determine the policy
options.C: Delegation of legislative power is constitutionally impermissible in all circumstances; the Oireachtas must always determine every rule of law by primary statute
options.D: Delegation is constitutionally permissible provided the parent statute grants the delegated body the power to legislate 'as if' it were the Oireachtas, subject to negative resolution procedure

questions.explanation: The Cityview Press test requires that the primary legislation must contain the principles and policies guiding the delegated body's exercise of its delegated power. The delegated instrument (usually a ministerial statutory instrument) may flesh out the details, give operational effect to the policy, or fill in technical gaps — but it cannot itself determine the fundamental policy. This derives from Article 15.2.1 degrees: since the Oireachtas has the sole and exclusive law-making power, it cannot entirely hand that power to another body. However, recognising practical necessity, the courts allow delegation of rule-making power provided the legislature retains the policy-determining function. Regulations that go beyond giving effect to principles and policies and themselves determine policy are ultra vires the parent statute and unconstitutional.

questions.question: Laurentiu v Minister for Justice [1999] 4 IR 26 (Supreme Court) involved a challenge to Section 5(1)(e) of the Aliens Act 1935, which empowered the Minister to make orders for the deportation of aliens 'in such manner as the Minister may determine'. The Supreme Court's decision has significant implications for delegated legislation. Which of the following most accurately states the outcome?

options.A: The court upheld the provision, holding that the broad ministerial discretion was constitutionally valid because deportation is an inherently executive function to which the Cityview Press principles and policies test does not apply
options.B: The court struck down the provision as an unconstitutional delegation because it contained no principles or policies to guide the Minister's discretion, effectively giving the Minister a free-standing legislative power over deportation
options.C: The court upheld the provision by reading it restrictively to limit the Minister's powers to deportation ordered by a court, thereby importing a judicially supervised element
options.D: The court held the provision was a Henry VIII clause — constitutionally valid because it merely allowed the Minister to update the primary legislation's procedural provisions without altering the substantive law

questions.explanation: In Laurentiu v Minister for Justice [1999] 4 IR 26, the Supreme Court (Hamilton CJ, O'Flaherty, Barrington, Lynch, Keane JJ) struck down s.5(1)(e) of the Aliens Act 1935 as an unconstitutional delegation of legislative power. The provision gave the Minister a virtually unconstrained discretion to determine the deportation of aliens 'as the Minister may determine' — there were no principles or policies in the Act to guide the exercise of this discretion. This amounted to delegating to the Minister the power to determine the fundamental policy on deportation, which is a core legislative function that Article 15.2.1 degrees reserves to the Oireachtas. Laurentiu is a significant practical authority: it led to the enactment of the Immigration Act 1999 to provide the required legislative framework.

questions.question: A 'Henry VIII clause' in Irish legislative drafting refers to a statutory provision that empowers a minister to amend primary legislation by secondary instrument. Which of the following most accurately states the Irish constitutional position on the validity of such clauses?

options.A: Henry VIII clauses are constitutionally valid in all circumstances, as they involve the Oireachtas voluntarily delegating its power rather than an executive usurpation of legislative power
options.B: Henry VIII clauses are constitutionally invalid per se because they permit an executive officer to exercise what is in substance a power to amend or repeal Acts of the Oireachtas, which is exclusively a legislative function
options.C: Henry VIII clauses are constitutionally permissible only where the purpose is to adapt legislation to EU law requirements, pursuant to the constitutional mandate in Article 29.4
options.D: Henry VIII clauses are constitutionally suspect and subject to strict scrutiny: they may be valid if sufficiently limited and purpose-specific, but a broadly drafted clause permitting substantive amendment of primary legislation will be struck down under the Cityview Press principles and policies test

questions.explanation: Henry VIII clauses occupy contested constitutional territory in Ireland. They are not per se unconstitutional (the courts have upheld narrowly defined adaptation clauses, particularly for EU law purposes), but broadly drafted clauses that permit ministerial amendment of the substantive provisions of primary legislation will fail the Cityview Press test: amending primary legislation is quintessentially a legislative function that Articles 15.2 and 15.4 assign to the Oireachtas. The European Communities Act 1972, s.3 is the best-known example of a constitutionally authorised Henry VIII clause (permitted by the constitutional mandate to implement EU law under Art. 29.4), and its constitutionality was upheld in cases like Meagher v Minister for Agriculture [1994] 1 IR 329 precisely because of the specific EU law mandate.

questions.question: Article 15.12 and 15.13 of the Constitution confer privileges on members of the Oireachtas. Which of the following correctly distinguishes between these two provisions?

options.A: Article 15.12 grants absolute privilege for statements made in either House; Article 15.13 grants qualified privilege for statements made outside the Houses in the course of parliamentary duties
options.B: Article 15.12 grants absolute privilege for utterances made in either House or at any committee of either House; Article 15.13 exempts members from arrest when travelling to, within, or returning from the Houses (except in cases of treason, felony, or breach of the peace)
options.C: Article 15.12 protects members from civil liability for statements made in Dail Eireann only; Article 15.13 extends this protection to statements made in committee and to senators
options.D: Article 15.12 grants members immunity from criminal prosecution for statements in either House; Article 15.13 grants immunity from civil liability for the same statements

questions.explanation: Article 15.12 provides that each member of each House has absolute privilege for any utterance in either House or any committee of either House — this is a constitutional guarantee of free speech in parliament, ensuring members can speak without fear of defamation actions (and without fear of criminal prosecution for the content of their contributions). It is absolute: no matter how false or defamatory the statement, a member cannot be sued or prosecuted. Article 15.13 provides members with exemption from arrest while going to, coming from, or being within either House, except in cases of treason, felony or breach of the peace. These are separate privileges: 15.12 is about freedom of speech; 15.13 is about freedom of movement for parliamentary purposes.

questions.question: In Maguire v Ardagh [2002] 1 IR 385 (the Abbeylara case), the Supreme Court considered the powers of an Oireachtas sub-committee investigating the fatal shooting of John Carty by the Garda Emergency Response Unit. The majority held that the sub-committee had exceeded its constitutional powers. Which of the following most accurately states the principle established?

options.A: The Oireachtas has no power to investigate the conduct of state officials in the exercise of their constitutional functions; all such investigations must be conducted by a tribunal of inquiry chaired by a High Court judge
options.B: An Oireachtas committee cannot make findings or express conclusions that would be equivalent to determining the guilt, innocence, or culpability of named individuals in respect of specific incidents — that is a judicial function under Article 34.1
options.C: The Oireachtas sub-committee lacked jurisdiction because the shooting occurred before a formal committee resolution was passed authorising the investigation, rendering all subsequent proceedings ultra vires
options.D: Oireachtas committees may investigate matters touching on individual conduct only where the individual concerned is a member of the Oireachtas; investigation of ordinary citizens is constitutionally impermissible

questions.explanation: In Maguire v Ardagh [2002] 1 IR 385 (Abbeylara), the Supreme Court held that the Oireachtas sub-committee on security had no constitutional power to make findings of fact that would be equivalent to a determination of the culpability of named individuals (the Garda officers involved in the Abbeylara shooting). Such determinations would be tantamount to exercising a judicial function — adjudicating on the legal rights and liabilities of identified persons — which is reserved to the courts by Article 34.1. The Oireachtas's investigative powers are extensive but must remain within the legislative sphere: committees can investigate systemic failures, policy matters, and institutional conduct, but cannot make individual culpability findings equivalent to a judicial verdict. This case significantly constrained the scope of parliamentary inquiries.

questions.question: Article 15.3.1 degrees provides that each House of the Oireachtas shall make its own rules and standing orders for the regulation of its own business. An opposition TD challenges a new standing order on the ground that it was adopted in a manner that breaches constitutional fairness requirements. Which of the following most accurately states the court's likely approach?

options.A: The court will readily intervene to strike down the standing order, as Article 15.3.1 degrees must be read subject to the constitutional guarantee of fair procedures in Article 40.3
options.B: The court will refuse to intervene as a matter of separation of powers: the internal regulation of each House's business is a matter within its exclusive constitutional competence, and courts will not generally review standing orders for procedural compliance
options.C: The court will conduct a proportionality review to determine whether the standing order constitutes a disproportionate restriction on the right of elected members to participate in the legislative process
options.D: The court will refer the matter to the Article 26 procedure for an advisory opinion from the Supreme Court before deciding whether to intervene

questions.explanation: Article 15.3.1 degrees confers an exclusive constitutional power on each House to regulate its own procedures through standing orders. Courts apply the principle of non-interference with the internal proceedings of the Oireachtas as a matter of separation of powers: the legislative organ must be free to organise its own affairs without judicial supervision of its procedural rules. The courts have held that they will not review the validity of standing orders or the conduct of debates within the Houses. This principle reflects the constitutional independence of the legislature in its internal governance. It is an application of the broader principle that each organ of state has exclusive jurisdiction over its own constitutional functions. The exception (not yet arisen in Ireland) would be if a standing order directly violated a specific and clear constitutional requirement.

questions.question: Article 17.2 of the Constitution requires a 'money message' from the Government before the Dail can pass legislation involving the appropriation of revenue or other public moneys. Which of the following scenarios most clearly engages this requirement?

options.A: A private member's Bill proposing to reduce the rate of income tax; this does not require a money message as it reduces rather than appropriates public revenue
options.B: A government Bill providing for a new form of ministerial order with associated procedural requirements but no express expenditure provisions
options.C: A private member's Bill establishing a new statutory body with functions requiring state expenditure on staff and offices, to be funded from the Central Fund
options.D: A government Bill amending the Companies Act to require directors of larger companies to hold professional qualifications, with associated registration fees payable to the CRO

questions.explanation: Article 17.2 requires a Government money message before the Dail can pass any vote or enact any law involving the appropriation of revenue or public moneys. A private member's Bill establishing a new statutory body funded from the Central Fund would require such a message: it involves the appropriation of public funds (staff, offices, operational costs). Without the money message, the Bill cannot constitutionally proceed. This reflects the constitutional allocation of fiscal initiative to the executive: the Government controls public expenditure. Option A (reducing tax) reduces revenue but is not an appropriation in the sense of Art. 17.2. Option D involves fees, which are not an appropriation of existing public moneys. The money message requirement has been a significant practical constraint on private members' legislative initiatives.

questions.question: Meagher v Minister for Agriculture [1994] 1 IR 329 is the leading authority on the constitutional validity of regulations made under the European Communities Act 1972, s.3(3), which permits ministers to make regulations having the force of law to implement EU obligations. Which constitutional challenge was raised and how did the Supreme Court respond?

options.A: The challenge alleged that s.3(3) was a Henry VIII clause and therefore an unconstitutional delegation of legislative power; the Supreme Court struck it down, requiring all EU obligations to be implemented by primary legislation
options.B: The challenge alleged that s.3(3) was an unconstitutional delegation of legislative power under Article 15.2.1 degrees; the Supreme Court upheld its validity on the basis of the constitutional mandate in Article 29.4 to fulfil EU obligations, which displaces the usual Cityview Press analysis
options.C: The challenge alleged that EU regulations had become part of Irish domestic law without the consent of the Oireachtas, in violation of Article 15.2.1 degrees; the court upheld this argument and required all EU secondary legislation to be incorporated by Irish statute
options.D: The challenge alleged that ministerial implementation of EU directives without Oireachtas scrutiny violated Article 15.3 (rules and standing orders); the court upheld the challenge and required a parliamentary committee to review all such regulations

questions.explanation: In Meagher v Minister for Agriculture [1994] 1 IR 329, the Supreme Court upheld the validity of s.3(3) of the European Communities Act 1972, which allows ministers to make regulations implementing EU obligations with the force of law. The constitutional challenge argued this was an unconstitutional delegation of legislative power under Art. 15.2.1 degrees. The court held that the usual Cityview Press 'principles and policies' analysis is modified in the EU law context: Article 29.4 of the Constitution (as amended to permit EU membership) provides a specific constitutional mandate to fulfil Community obligations, which includes a mandate to implement EU law by ministerial regulation where necessary. The EU context thus effectively authorises a broader delegation than would be permissible for purely domestic legislative matters.

questions.question: Article 15.6.1 degrees of the Constitution provides that the right to raise and maintain military or armed forces is vested exclusively in the Oireachtas. If the Government were to establish a paramilitary-style agency to perform security functions without legislative authorisation, which constitutional provisions would most directly be violated?

options.A: Articles 28.2 (executive power) and 40.6.1 degrees(ii) (freedom of assembly), because the Government was operating an unofficial assembly in violation of citizens' associative rights
options.B: Articles 15.6.1 degrees (exclusive Oireachtas power over military forces) and 15.2.1 degrees (sole and exclusive legislative power), because the executive would be performing an act reserved to the legislature
options.C: Article 34.1 (justice administered in courts) and 40.4 (habeas corpus), because an unauthorised security agency could deprive citizens of liberty without court order
options.D: Article 28.3 (war powers) and Article 15.6.1 degrees, because establishing a military force without Oireachtas authorisation is equivalent to declaring war

questions.explanation: Article 15.6.1 degrees explicitly states: 'The right to raise and maintain military or armed forces is vested exclusively in the Oireachtas.' A government-established paramilitary agency without statutory authorisation would directly violate this provision, which places the power to maintain armed forces in the legislature — not the executive — precisely to prevent executive overreach. It would also violate Art. 15.2.1 degrees because establishing such an agency involves the exercise of a function that requires legislative authorisation. This provision reflects a core concern of the constitutional framers: that the executive should not have autonomous military capacity. The Garda Siochana, the Defence Forces, and all security bodies must therefore be established and governed by Acts of the Oireachtas, not executive order.
